name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Specs:
RX 9070
Ryzen 5600
32 GB RAM
Installed on 2TB SSD

I've only played Warzone's Casual Battle Royale (150 + bots) and I am enjoying it. However, the game has problems that do hinder the experience.

The short version is if you like COD, have a decent rig, and have friends who want to play Warzone then download and play it. Otherwise you're better off playing something else.

Good:
- Gameplay is fast
- Loads of weapons
- Challenges to do in-between shootouts
- Lots of customisation
- Vehicles speed up traversing the map
- Plenty of unique battlegrounds
- Multiple ways to respawn/revive

Cons:
- Performance is sluggish (all low settings and not getting higher than 110fps)
- Slow load times between matches and respawn states (Running on SSD)
- Map is too big
- Lots of nothing going on
- Game heavily leans on mid to long-range combat so weapons like shotguns are underwhelming
- Grenades and abilities feel useless
- Time to kill is inconsistent
- Challenges are basic 'go here' or 'wait here' missions
- Over 150GB's of storage required (even if only playing warzone)
- Extremely messy and cluttered UI
- Store is completely pointless and full of awful skins

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty is still a very fun game to play with friends. Multiplayer is good, Zombies an absolute blast to play, and Warzone can still be enjoyable. However, there are some things that drag the overall experience down, such as AI-generated assets being used in the game, aggressive monetization, the strict online-only requirement and the game overall feeling stagnant currently.

In addition, the game refuses to function under Proton, making it impossible to play on SteamOS-based handhelds and Linux desktops. Warzone and Black Ops 7 will also soon require "Secure Boot" to be enabled, which sounds like a good idea on paper, but it will make it more difficult for people to dual-boot with certain distros and it will kill off support entirely for older machines that could otherwise run the game just fine.

If some of these things were resolved, I would easily give this game a positive rating, but it is hard to in its current state.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
I’ve put in over 470 hours, and Call of Duty® continues to deliver a mix of fast-paced action, satisfying gunplay, and constant updates that keep things fresh. The maps are diverse, the weapon customization is deep, and when the servers run smooth, the experience is intense and addicting.

I’ve been playing first-person shooters since I was about 8 years old, and I’ve gone through so many of these games over the years. Call of Duty has always stood out as one of my favorites. I may not be a fan of the real world’s chaos, but in-game, it’s pure fun — an escape that lets me focus on skill, teamwork, and progression.

That said, it’s not without flaws. Performance issues, occasional bugs, and balancing problems do creep in, and the skill-based matchmaking can sometimes make casual sessions feel sweaty. The grind can also feel heavy at times if you’re chasing every unlock.

Still, at its core, this is a solid FPS that rewards time, practice, and dedication. Whether you’re jumping in solo, squadding up with friends, or grinding ranked matches, there’s always something to chase and improve on.

If you’re into competitive shooters, it’s worth the hours — I’d definitely recommend it. I very proud of Call of Duty are starting out new movie and yep I gonna watch it! 

I hope you enjoy this game the way I truly mean it, just like I do. See you all in there, Call of Duty fans —you’ll never know when I might drop in. And when I do… better be ready, because when I drop in, it’s always a surprise you won’t forget.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
I’ve been a long-time CoD fan, especially of the Black Ops era, but the current state of the franchise is nothing short of toxic. The core gunplay still has potential, yet the entire experience is ruined by manipulative design choices that make the game feel more like a psychological trap than a fair competitive shooter.

Matchmaking Hell (SBMM/EBMM):
The game constantly throws me into lobbies where my team plays like bots while the enemy team is stacked with highly skilled players. It doesn’t matter how well I perform; the system deliberately handicaps me. Even when I carry with insane numbers or earn a Kill Chain, the outcome is already rigged by bad teammates.

Shotguns and snipers—my main weapons—become a joke. I can land a perfect point-blank shot, and it registers as a hitmarker while my opponent walks away untouched. Meanwhile, enemies melt me instantly. This isn’t bad aim—it’s intentional netcode manipulation and engagement balancing.

Despite top performance, I rarely get Play of the Game. Wins are scarce not because I underperform, but because the system keeps punishing me for doing well. After hours of grinding, maybe one match feels enjoyable, while the rest are pure frustration.

The game no longer respects player skill or time. It manipulates outcomes to keep you hooked, cycling you through loss streaks and rare “dopamine” wins. This is gambling mechanics disguised as matchmaking.

Final Verdict

CoD has become unplayable, unfair, and unethical. It’s no longer about skill or competition; it’s about being controlled by an algorithm that decides whether you’re allowed to enjoy the game.

I’m done. Like many others, I’d rather quit than continue supporting a franchise that punishes its most dedicated players.

⭐ Rating: 2/10 – fun gunplay buried under one of the dirtiest matchmaking systems in gaming.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
I’ve played Warzone since launch, and it has officially become one of the most frustrating games out there. The matchmaking is completely broken—instead of fair lobbies, you get thrown into sweat-filled matches against players way above your skill level, making the game feel more like torture than fun on top of that every lobby you will across cheaters.

And don’t even get me started on controller aim assist. It’s basically an aimbot that gives controller players a ridiculous advantage over mouse & keyboard. The game claims to be “crossplay friendly,” but it’s laughable when controller players can beam across the map with sticky aim assist while PC players have to fight recoil, tracking, and movement without any help.

The developers clearly don’t care about balancing the experience. Every update promises “fixes,” yet the core problems never get addressed. Instead, they pump out new skins and microtransactions while the actual gameplay suffers.

At this point, Warzone feels less like a competitive shooter and more like a rigged casino where the odds are stacked against you. Until they fix unfair SBMM and tone down aim assist abuse, this game is nothing more than a broken mess pretending to be an esport.

⭐ Rating: 1/5 – Only fun if you enjoy unfair lobbies and getting outgunned by controller aimbots.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
TL;DR - Games like BO6 are on Life Support and are dead and gone the moment Activision disable the Servers, due to forced Texture Streaming, meaning even the Singleplayer will be completely unplayable, unless the Game is patched. 
Be aware, your game might be DOA


I don't see many others mentioning this - and it remains to be seen if it will be the case for BO7 as well - but I'd personally be VERY worried about the fact that Texture Streaming (streaming highres textures from Activision's Servers so as to make the game less bloated) might be becoming mandatory for CoD. The reason this is worrying - aside from having to always be online - is the simple fact that that means that the day Activision shut down the Servers these games, Singleplayer and all, are dead. Six feet under, pushing up Daysies to forever be remembered, but not played. 

Considering the impact SKG had on the whole Gaming scene, I genuinely expected to see more people throwing a fuss about this, but I haven't seen any big figures covering it. And I don't have much reason to believe that BO6 was the exception. 

So yeah, steer clear, because Call of Duty might just become a giant Graveyard for Content. Seems to me that Activision didn't learn much from Destiny 2 and its content vaulting.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
(Reviewing BO6 not WZ) The gunplay, movement, and aesthetics are all peak and I'm hyped for BO7. That being said, there is a HUGE problem with console players using Cronus/Strikepacks and it's honestly pathetic how common they have become. 99% of my lobbies will have 10 out of 12 players on console with one or two of them blatantly using keyboard + mouse with aim assist or controller with aim assist that locks onto you like it does in GTA.

Unfortunately you cannot disable crossplay on PC as far as I know so we are stuck with them, they rarely ever get banned on top of that because of how subtle those cheat devices are. Wallhackers are pretty common too but harder to identify because good gamesense is easily mistakable for walls, it's a tossup whether somebody is just listening to footsteps or walling. If they have the trademark Cronus aim assist then it is easier to tell.

Gamepass has almost ruined this game IMO because it lowered the cost of entry to less than $5. name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
[b]Note: This is written from the perspective of someone who dabbled in Fortnite when it first came out, Warzone for a few months in 2020 and Rainbow for about a year, but aside from that has never played shooters consistently, or well. [/b]

As someone who didn't grow up liking shooters due to my severe inability to aim, this was the first time my friends convinced me to buy one and play with them. I dumped the cash and played through the single-player first.

I absolutely loved it. 
I loved the characters (though I wish they fleshed out some of their backgrounds more) and the interactive nature of the base home you stayed at. The little puzzles, the stealth, the story itself--it was all unique to me, and I appreciated just walking through some of the areas to see how far I could push the game. The gun play was forgiving, even on harder modes, which allowed me to not get frustrated or heated. And the environments were just nice to look at.

The multiplayer was a different experience, but I enjoyed practicing and seeing my aim and overall skill improve over time. I liked the guns, I appreciated that the overall lack of recoil on a lot of the guns made it hard to blame anything except my own aim. I enjoyed the struggle of being bad at games because I knew how to improve. Seeing my guns unlock cool camos and add-ons was satisfying. 

Warzone was the same as always, and it was nice when Verdansk came back, the map I originally played in. Dying to snipers sucks as always.


My main issues with this game, which is similar to most folks, is the amount of space this game takes up. Aside from that, I'm not a fan of the ridiculous skins that have come out, and the absurdity of announcing BO7 less than a year after 6's release.

[u]The Software Itself[/u]
I have had to buy a new hard drive for this game. I've personally never had a severe crash more than once or twice, but have had semi-consistent graphic bugs (probably cuz I have a GEFORCE 1080).

[u]The Skins[/u]
The skins that have come out since launch have gotten increasingly unserious, and I'm not trying to pretend that this game has to be 100% gritty and militaristic, but I refuse to accept death from the hands of Beavis and Butthead. This is a game about humans, people from military organizations. It completely rips you out of any kind of immersion, and more than that, it just looks like the cheap money grab that it is. I have a handful of skins, but at the very least they keep operators realistic, human, and wearing nothing more than a funky outfit or cool shades. 

[u]Black Ops 7[/u]
I can't say much about BO7's (personally) premature announcement other than it feeling like an absolute spit in my face after dropping so many hours and dollars on this game. Everyone knows that their efforts are going to the new game, and it won't be long until 6 is completely thrown to the side. The worst part is, I know everyone is going to buy 7 because they know that. 

Admittedly, it's sad to see a company that seemingly has a bad track record with its community and truly listening to its desires for the game. 
But, I have, and still do, enjoy this game heavily, and it is the first time I've spent this long on any mainstream game. It scratches a particular itch in my brain. So anyone that's new to this franchise, enjoy it as much as you can. Deal with the hard drive sacrifice, and have fun. 
I am planning on going back to MW1-3. I hear they're great.

At the end of the day we're all addicted and need help but hey, it is what it is.

I’ve really enjoyed my time playing Call of Duty®. What I like the most is how smooth and fast the gameplay feels—it’s easy to pick up but still challenging enough to keep me hooked. The campaign is exciting and cinematic, while the multiplayer is where the game truly shines with its variety of modes and constant action.

Of course, there are a few things that could be better. Sometimes the game feels a bit repetitive, especially if you’ve played older Call of Duty titles. Also, the extra content and cosmetics can be a bit much at times.

Overall, though, I’d definitely recommend Call of Duty® to anyone who enjoys first-person shooters. It’s fun, polished, and has a strong community that keeps the experience alive.

Skins are blatantly pay-to-win because they’re impossible to see in the visual diarrhea Activision calls maps. The TTK is so pathetically low you drop dead before you even know you’re being shot. Call of Duty (HQ) presents itself as a centralized hub for the franchise, but instead of providing convenience, it creates frustration. By forcing all entries into one bloated installation, it consumes enormous amounts of storage space unnecessarily. This approach undermines accessibility and punishes players who only wish to engage with one specific title in the series. Rather than streamlining the experience, it complicates it.
Visually, the franchise has shifted away from its signature gritty realism toward bright, cartoonish aesthetics. Skins and cosmetic packs now dominate the presentation, often resembling the style of games like Fortnite rather than maintaining Call of Duty’s military authenticity. This departure in tone alienates long-time fans and erodes the visual identity that helped define the series. Even the more detailed environments are overshadowed by the jarring, unrealistic designs of character models.
Overall, Call of Duty (HQ) is a misstep that damages the franchise’s identity, convenience, and appeal. By forcing massive installations, undermining story delivery, weakening gameplay flow, and adopting a misguided visual direction, it strips away much of what made Call of Duty unique. Instead of enhancing the player experience, it clutters and detracts from it. The result is a platform that feels unnecessary, intrusive, and disconnected from the series’ roots.
Call of Duty (HQ) gets a 1/10.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review: Pretty much bloatware at this point. Selling proper main-line entries as DLC, really?! Goes without saying that the crossover, kiddie "Fortnite-ification" of the series (and scummy monetization DLC overload) is massively stupid and tone-breaking. Black Ops 6 is massively overpriced, doesn't run well, and is filled w/ sweats, hackers, and is generally un-fun to play when you constantly get 360 no-scoped dolphin-dived w/ the new movement system. MW3 (Reboot) was so on-rails w/ horrendous pacing & dialogue that I couldn't even finish it. If you want to play a Call of Duty, I'd suggest Call of Duty 2, World at War, Black Ops 1, Black Ops 2, and maybe Modern Warfare (2019). The rest I either haven't played or are straight-up terrible.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty: Warzone delivers one of the most exciting battle royale experiences available. The gunplay is crisp and satisfying, with a wide variety of weapons and loadouts to match different playstyles. The Gulag mechanic and large, detailed maps add tension and keep matches feeling fresh and unpredictable.

Playing with friends is where the game really shines—there’s never a dull moment, and squad coordination can turn close fights into unforgettable wins. Regular updates and seasonal content also keep things interesting.

That said, the game isn’t perfect. Balancing issues pop up now and then, and the download size can be a hurdle. But despite those drawbacks, the core experience remains incredibly fun and worth the time.

Verdict: ★★★★☆ – A thrilling, action-packed shooter that nails its gameplay, even if it has a few rough edges.

But until they get back in touch with the community and everything that made CoD a successful franchise in the first place. I cannot recommend this game anymore to everyone and to anyone who is considering BO7 I would stay far away and invest your time and money into something else that actually respects your time and money, especially for a hobby like gaming.

Fun: 420/69 (would rage again)

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Once upon a time, Call of Duty was the gold standard for first-person shooters — a franchise that delivered unforgettable campaigns, tight multiplayer maps, and a sense of community that kept you coming back night after night. Those days are gone. What we have now is a bloated, soulless product wearing the skin of a once-great series.
The gameplay feels like it’s been on autopilot for years — recycled mechanics, uninspired map design, and a constant push toward flashy cosmetics instead of meaningful innovation. The heart-pounding, skill-based matches of the past have been replaced with a grind designed to keep you spending, not playing.
And let’s talk about the elephant in the room: microtransactions. Every corner of the game is plastered with overpriced skins, weapon blueprints, and battle passes. It’s no longer about unlocking rewards through skill or dedication — it’s about how much you’re willing to pay. The greed is palpable, and it’s suffocating what little fun remains.
No matter how many flashy trailers or seasonal updates they push out, Call of Duty will never recapture the magic it had in its prime. The soul of the series has been traded for profit margins, and the result is a game that feels more like a storefront than a battlefield.
Verdict: If you’re chasing the glory days, you won’t find them here. This isn’t the Call of Duty you fell in love with — it’s a corporate cash grab wearing its name.

[b]Every[/b] single 'good' moment I have had in my 1.3K+ hours spent playing Black Ops 6 / Warzone (with a tiny smidge of Zombies) would have been easily better had it occurred in another game, either by having the same gameplay situation play out, or simply by playing with the same friends. Every. Single. One. 

The UI/UX is so atrociously bad that every single person who was responsible for it being released and continuing to exist in this state should be truly, DEEPLY ashamed of themselves. 

Every single moment spent outside of an active match is a processional masterclass in "Get it done. Yesterday. Last week. No time to fix this or go back to that. Is the new thing added? Get to work on the next content update. We need to sell more. SELL MORE!", and it's an agonising, sickening shame.

Inside a match? There is some fun to be had, but frustrations and annoyances still abound.
There are so many small easy win improvements that could make the game so much more enjoyable... if only the game's purpose was to be good, rather than maximally profitable.

Whether it be the stark contrast in attention to detail between elements of the game looking to push a sale vs everything else, the CONSTANT bombardment of ads for paid content, questionable / inconsistent / incoherent balancing, the disgusting frequency of glitches/bugs ranging from trivial through annoying to outright broken, blatant aimbotters are still not uncommon despite using invasive and compatibility-breaking kernel 'anti-cheat', the server-side heuristic behavioural anti-cheat engine's high false positive rate, or any of a truckload more things besides, nothing here sparks joy. Least of all the notoriously [b]  T O X I C  [/b] playerbase, far too many of them doing their best to live up to the reputation.

Absolutely nothing makes me like, or want to play this era of COD beyond its use as a no effort zero brain distraction and dopamine grind of the FPS(/BR) variety. Such is the potency of its core game loop optimisation (nvm the predatory psychological manipulation). Well, that, and spite. Because I put in the hours to [b]know[/b] what I'm talking about in saying

[h1]This is a trash game[/h1] [h2]for trash people[/h2] [h3]or people who don't know any better[/h3].

[h2]Your time, money, and attention are better spent elsewhere[/h2]

The fact that multiple parts of the BF6 open beta were already SIGNIFICANTLY more polished than the current COD retail experience make it unlikely I'd be coming back to COD after BF6 drops, but since the majority of fps gamers I know have already said they're going to BF6 (including every new friend met through COD), that chance drops to absolutely zero.

And that sparks joy.

Unlike this game.

Do yourself all the favours:
Do ANYTHING else with your time.

And be better to one another.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
I've been a huge call of duty fan for years, played every single game since World at War. But every game, the bar drops lower and lower, and I honestly can't justify paying for this game any longer. Only for people who have time and lots of extra money. You pay for a game that has dozens of extra paywalls inside, guns locked behind battle passes and challenges that you need to do a ton of work to get (which you can just unlock and use by paying for it), and a pretty bad cheating epidemic. I feel sorry for the people who love the series, but it has unfortunately failed us, once again.

The gameplay loop is fun, truthfully- immediately stale after a few games. There is no experience, no grit, just rainbow skins and repetition. There should be an option to have skins hidden and replaced by random mil-sim operators, but you instead are forced to look at rainbow vomit constantly.

Campaign's story was interesting but not nearly as exciting as it could've been. It seemed to be a somewhat repetitive advancement of the BOCW storyline, following a lot of the same story beats and lacking the feel it had.

Guns: 6.5/10 - Too wobbly, always the same
Gameplay: 7.3 - New and somewhat interesting, but quickly repetitive
Style: 2/10 - Constant stupidity with skins and cosmetics
Story: 5:10 - Somewhat boring and stale but does have its moments
Overall: 5.2/10 - Not worth the hard earned cash

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
After over 500 hours in Call of Duty: Warzone, I can confidently say this game has earned its place as a go-to for both casual play and competitive action. It’s the perfect game to sit back and unwind at the end of a long day, yet it still delivers intense, heart-pounding moments that keep you coming back.

What I love most is how even casual matches can escalate into high-stakes battles as the circle closes. There’s a unique thrill in watching a game that starts relaxed transform into a tense, strategic showdown. Every encounter feels meaningful, and there’s a satisfying sense of progression whether you’re honing your loadout or learning new map strategies.

The game also balances accessibility with depth. New players can jump in and enjoy the core experience, while veterans can dive into the competitive mechanics, from advanced movement and weapon mastery to tactical decision-making under pressure.

Overall, Warzone is not just a game but a dynamic experience, a blend of strategy, reflex, and fun that keeps me coming back for months on end. Whether you’re in it for casual drops or adrenaline-fueled endgame fights, Warzone delivers consistently.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty has become a mess of greed, bugs, and AI. They use AI-generated art (like the “Necroclaus” screen and six-fingered glove) without disclosure at the time, replacing real artists entirely. Forced cross-play traps PC players against console users who can opt out, leading to constant corner-camping and unfair matches.
Performance? A disaster. VRAM leaks chew through 8GB+ cards until you crash. Random DirectX errors boot you mid-match despite perfect setups. Stutters, freezes, and optimisation failures plague even high-end rigs.

Gameplay: 
Dragon-breath Shotgun Players turns every match into a sweat-fest or a slaughter. The community’s toxic, cheaters slip through, and devs respond with canned “update your drivers” nonsense while pumping out overpriced cosmetics—most AI-made.
Then there’s BlackCell: $25+ a month for a compounding grind boost 10% - 15% added on.
This franchise doesn’t respect players’ time, money, or hardware. It’s a cash-grab live service rotting from the inside.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
I’ve been a long-time Call of Duty fan, especially of the Black Ops series, but it’s hard to ignore how far the franchise has fallen. Black Ops 2 and the games before it felt like they were made with actual care—for the gameplay, the story, the challenge. Black Ops 6? It feels like a storefront first and a game second.

Let’s talk microtransactions. The game costs full price, but nearly every menu is pushing overpriced bundles—$20 to $50 for ridiculous, cartoonish skins that completely break immersion. I didn’t buy Black Ops to see American Dad, Squid Game, or unicorn skins with rainbow particle effects. It’s like they want to turn this into Call of Duty: Fortnite Edition. (If you look at the American Dad - Rogers bundle , and inspect him doing HIS emote of drinking alcohol - They couldn't even be bothered to align it properly.)

Zombies mode, once a highlight of the series, has been dumbed down significantly. The maps are big but feel soulless, and the Easter eggs—once clever and challenging puzzles that felt rewarding—are now simplified and barely worth the effort. You can survive into high rounds with minimal effort, and the tension just isn’t there anymore.

It’s clear this game is built to keep people in the store, not in the gameplay.

If you’re a longtime fan hoping for a return to form—this isn’t it. Feels more like a cash grab wrapped in nostalgia.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
The Call of Duty we once knew and loved has died. 

Each year its the same thing, except instead of a new COD, we get a new 80GB package, that will just eat up all the space on your computer until you are forced to upgrade your storage. Im sick and tired of this cycle. 

If your friends are peer pressuring you into buying this game, im telling you its not worth it. This game sucks. "Skill based matchmaking" is a joke. Cheaters run rampant. Activision only cares about you eating their slop, they want you addicted so they can keep sucking your money dry. 

If you value your time, DONT buy this game. 
If you value your money, DONT buy this game. 
If you value having a good gaming experience, DONT BUY THIS GAME. 

It is about the only thing you can do to tell the devs & publishers that you don't want a repeat multiplayer game, with a hastily written campaign, boring mechanics and a non immersive experience.

To anyone at COD who still cares about the game, please for the love of all that is holy, take 5.

Listen to your community, listen to the feedback. 

Its clear that not everyone wants a battle royale, not everyone wants to see whatever microtransaction garbage you call content. Im sick of it. Im sure so many people are too.

Bring back old DLC packs, not season passes. that gate keep and lock content if you dont play enough. Im willing to pay $100 for a game if the game has content that I can still play. 

I hate to bring it up, but genuinely BF6 is doing something COD would never think of doing again... 

Giving a reason for players to keep coming back. 

They said they were not worried about BF6, and that tells me that COD, is just too far gone.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty's current state is a major disappointment and the game is dead. It's plagued by issues that seem to be pushing the player base away.

Hackers and "Sweats": The game is full of cheaters, and the constant, intense lobbies make it difficult for casual players to have fun.

Skill-Based Matchmaking (SBMM): This system has created a frustrating and seemingly unfair experience. It often feels like the game actively works against you, with lower-skilled players getting what feels like a boost to even out the playing field.

Rigged Gameplay: The flow of matches often feels manipulated. It's as if the game gives you a "mercy win" after a long losing streak to encourage you to keep playing.

Microtransactions: The focus on selling in-game items has taken priority over delivering a high-quality, balanced game.

I remember when Call of Duty was celebrated for its pure, competitive gameplay. The experience now feels rigged and focused on monetization rather than skill.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty started as a great mil-sim FPS that competed with Battlefield, Medal of Honor, America’s Army, and Arma. Today it competes against arcade shooters like Apex Legends and Fortnite. The movement is terrible. Jumping, sliding around, sliding sideways while shooting, proning out while shooting is just all way over the top. The skins, non-combat operators, are terrible. Guns that shoot bubbles and lightning bolts are terrible.

On top of that, the gameplay experience is frustrating in other ways. Skill-based matchmaking makes nearly every lobby sweaty and punishing, removing the casual fun that older CoDs were known for. Time-to-kill feels inconsistent, and weapon balance is almost always skewed toward a handful of “meta” guns while the rest of the arsenal feels useless. The store is pushed harder than the game itself, with overpriced bundles and constant cosmetic cash-grabs overshadowing the actual content. And when you finally get into a match, server issues, broken hit detection, and bugs only add to the frustration.

I think if the latest couple of CoD games were named as something different, they would do fine with whatever crowd they attract. As of now, I cannot recommend this game to anyone who expects CoD to be what CoD originally was.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty is still fun at its core. The gunplay feels tight, movement is solid, and when matches actually run smoothly, it’s some of the most addictive multiplayer you can get. But that’s where the praise ends.

The install size is absurd, 250–300GB+ for a multiplayer shooter is unacceptable. I shouldn’t have to sacrifice half my SSD just to play one mode. Updates are constant, massive, and often break more things than they fix.

SBMM makes casual play feel sweaty every game, while cheaters still slip through despite anti-cheat supposedly being 'improved.' Hit registration is inconsistent, dying behind cover is common, and servers feel unstable for a AAA title. Campaigns have been forgettable, basically glorified tutorials for Warzone.

It’s not a bad game, it’s just a bloated, mismanaged one. Strip down the file size, fix the netcode and anti-cheat, and actually listen to the community, then CoD could be great again. Until then, it’s fun for a few matches… before frustration sets back in.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Hello to both sides of Call of duty - the Average TV dinner low life loser that can play this game for hours on end and never get bored / always hungry for silly game play that's getting kinda disappointing / for those who actually have a heart and a few brain cells that run beautifully on what it means to play an actual good game (especially of COD). 

Most of call of duty has done the inevitable like a kid getting pushed down the hill in a shopping kart then after that he eats the ground and breaks his arm, expecting to be all in one piece. It's completely disgraceful to me as a die hard Call of Duty fan, and knowing the fact that your opponent is make a huge comeback in the scene of gaming and they - Battlefield 6 - are marking their territory on you guys and its honestly appealing to me that your competition is now owning you guys.

Now, knowing me I've been playing Call of duty as a young soldier waiting for the combat side of gaming to be my legacy and everything else. But As a guy who wants to see whats best for Call of duty, I've been very very very disappointed with them and haven't seen anything peak in CoD since 2015 (Black ops 3), now with that being said I can no longer fathom what is going on with the silly, dirty, stuck up, sadistic, meatball henchmen of the CoD teams (mainly fuqtivision).

It makes me wonder why the world wants to do this, its like going to watch the horror movie Abigail and just laughing the whole entire time expecting a jump scare when all I see from the horror movie is not scary but it's adorable in a terrible way. I got lost after they got rid of the OG warzone (despite them adding.. Kalahari desert from Mario kart...  my bad i mean Calrdera). and then they added all these new goofy little mechanics that changed my wonderful point of view on the game in warzone 2.0, that should of been a comparison to slimer rancher, i mean cmon acty, you guys are ACTing kinda silly now, pull yourselves together. (you can do it {:   ... ).

Now with that being said now we come down to - first off. I am dealing with a lot of sensitivity with my game, and its not like it normally has been, since season 5 came out, its been a huge embarrassment to the scenery, I mean yea like the characters, kinda but they are like... very irrelevant. (This is an owie for bme to say this) The skins are kinda copy and paste kinda like your international worst nightmare, Fortnite. I promised myself to not compare this game to fortnite, but you kinda did it to yourselves on that one. Cope and paste, terrible battle royale textures and for me its  like pri...I'm not gonna go there today,

Now for me its just feelinmg like you guys are lost in the production maze.. HEY ACTIVISION... DO YOU GUYS NEED A MAP FOR YOU AND YOUR PITY PARTIES YOU THROW ON COD? Or Are you ready to come out of your disgusting man cave and actually get yourselves into gear. You guys aren't getting any younger  I hope you know.

In conclusion there aren't any other things I should put out - even though I really want to - cause most of it is pretty straight forward from there. I will keep playing CoD but only games like Ghosts and Bo3 and CoD 4 MW. Till then I'm not interesting in the love triangle you guys are trying to stir me into... on thank you.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
I’ve put in more hours than I’d like to admit into CoD over the years, and honestly… it’s never been in a worse state. Support? Nonexistent. Try reaching out and you’ll either get a copy-paste bot response or no answer at all. It’s like Activision is actively allergic to fixing real problems.

Anti-cheat? Don’t make me laugh. It’s supposed to be "Ricochet," but it feels like it’s either go big or go home Valorant-style kernel-level intrusion OR absolutely nothing. Meanwhile, lobbies are a cesspool of wallhacks, aimbots, and closet cheaters who have been blatantly ruining games for months. The sheer number of cheaters is insane. I’ve played F2P garbage with better integrity.

And the player models/skins? What even is this game anymore? I signed up to play a military FPS, not Fortnite with Muppets. Cartoon operators, glow-in-the-dark skins, anime crossovers—it kills any kind of immersion. CoD used to feel like a shooter; now it’s a cash shop masquerading as one. Give me soldier models only, grounded and gritty, not whatever this clown show is supposed to be.

But the absolute cherry on top: communication bans in a rated M game. Why? It’s literally marketed as "intense violence, blood, gore, strong language." Yet somehow, me dropping a swear word or flaming a cheater is ban-worthy? It’s a joke. 

At this point, I’m convinced they don’t care about player experience at all.

name: Call of Duty: Modern Warfare II
review:
Call of Duty sucks now. This series used to be all about semi realistic military pvp.  We had meaningful campaigns, and online PvP was a uniform experience. 

Then esports and micro-transactions took off and COD has never been the same. Now we have skins that do not fit the in game world. People running around dressed up like ducks, zombies, nicki minaj, etc... Shooting bubbles from their smg's. 

Heck, some games didn't even give us a campaign. What happened to this franchise? 

World at War was peak. So was the original Modern Warfare trilogy. 

These games were great and I miss that rush these games used to offer. 

Unfortunately, as time has passed, you rarely find a cheat free lobby in these older titles.


I think the COD franchise needs to separate into different experiences, one for the ridiculous skin and event players, one for the traditional old heads.
